titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Kingman Brewster Personal Papers
titlestmt.author: compiled by Katherine Isham, Monika Lehman, Anna Ellsworth, Christine Weideman, and Alison Clemens

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Saturday January 11, 2020 at 08:02

did.abstract: The papers consist of correspondence, subject files, teaching and research materials, speeches, interviews, photographs, and printed material documenting the career of Kingman Brewster Jr.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Gift of Mary Louise Phillips Brewster, 1991. Gift of the Yale Alumni Magazine, 1992. Gift of Henry Chauncey, Jr., 1994. Transfer from the Office of the President, 1999.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The collection is open for research.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Copyright has been transferred to Yale University for unpublished materials authored or otherwise produced by the creator(s) of this collection. Copyright status for other collection materials is unknown. Transmission or reproduction of materials protected by U.S. Copyright Law (Title 17, U.S.C.) beyond that allowed by fair use requires the written permission of the copyright owners. Works not in the public domain cannot be commercially exploited without permission of the copyright owners. Responsibility for any use rests exclusively with the user.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Kingman Brewster Personal Papers (MS 572). Manuscripts and Archives, Yale University Library.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Kingman Brewster was born in Longmeadow, Massachusetts on June 17, 1919, the son of Kingman Brewster Sr. and Florence Foster. His parents divorced in 1923 and he and his sister settled with their mother in Cambridge, Massachusetts. His mother married Edward Ballentine, a Harvard University music professor. Brewster attended the Belmont Hill School, where he participated in debate and drama. Before entering Yale University as an undergraduate student, Brewster traveled to Europe with his family. He attended Yale College from 1937 to 1941, where he was chairman of the Yale Daily News and was involved in the America First campaign, protesting America's involvement in World War II.

After the attacks on Pearl Harbor, Brewster enlisted. He served as a Naval aviator and flew anti-submarine patrols in South America for three years. After the war ended, he attended Harvard Law School, where he served on the Harvard Law Review. He graduated magna cum laude in 1948. Following graduation, he went to Paris and served as assistant general counsel to Milton Katz, the United States Special Representative in Europe for the Economic Cooperation Administration. Thereafter he accepted a position in the economics department at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology. In 1950, Brewster was appointed assistant professor at Harvard Law School. He was promoted to full professor in 1953. While at Harvard, he became a noted expert on antitrust matters and international commerce and relations. His well-received publications included Antitrust and American Business Abroad, published in 1958, and The Law of International Transactions and Relations: Cases and Materials, co-authored with Milton Katz and published in 1960.

In 1960, Brewster returned to Yale University as provost under Yale president, A. Whitney Griswold, who had taught at Yale when Brewster was a student and was a friend of Brewster's parents. After Griswold died from cancer in 1963, Brewster became acting Yale University president and was named president in October. He was inaugurated in April 1964.

Brewster led the university through significant and controversial changes to the faculty, student body, and curricula. For a summary of the work of his presidential administration and the records that document it, see: Guide to the Kingman Brewster, Jr., President of Yale University, Records.

In May 1977, Brewster left Yale to become Ambassador to the Court of St. James's in the United Kingdom. He served as ambassador until 1981 and was well-liked by the British with whom he regularly interacted. He travelled throughout the country to meet people and noted that his job was to try "to advise my Government on British attitudes and concerns in the fullest way possible." After the ambassadorship ended, Brewster returned to New Haven, and worked for the New York-based law firm of Winthrop, Stimson, Putnam, and Roberts. He also served as chairman of the English Speaking Union of the United States, a group that sponsors cultural and educational opportunities for students and educators. He was active in other organizations, serving in positions with the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace, Reuters, Common Cause, and the American Council of Learned Societies. In 1984, he returned to London as resident partner for his law firm. In 1985, he was elected Master of University College at Oxford, an unusual position for an American to hold. He died at the age of 69 on November 8, 1988.

Brewster married Mary Louise Phillips in Jacksonville, Florida in 1942. Mary Louise was a Vassar College student and had met Brewster at a Yale football game. They had five children together: Kingman Brewster III, Constance, Deborah, Alden, and Riley.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The papers primarily document the personal and professional life of Brewster as a Harvard faculty member (1950-1960) and Ambassador to Great Britain (1977-1981). The papers also include informative (but limited) material from 1940 to 1950. The most substantive material in the collection is that created by Brewster himself. Correspondence, unpublished writings, speeches, and interviews, provide extensive documentation of his interests and expertise, including in the areas of the role of government; maintaining a viable center in the political opinion spectrum; American anti-trust laws; American companies doing business abroad; the role of a liberal arts higher education; Anglo-American relations; and the United States in world affairs. The documentation on Brewster as ambassador reflects the public side of his work. Internal Embassy discussions on issues or policies, planning for incident responses, and interactions with the US State Department, are not documented in the papers.
Records, including correspondence and speeches, documenting Brewster's tenures as Provost and President can be found in the Yale University Archives (see Related Materials note).

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The papers are arranged in six series: I. Correspondence, 1904-1984. II. Personal, 1866-1989. III. Professional, 1936-1989. IV. Writings, 1940-1988. V. Photographs, 1940-1988. VI. Clippings and Interviews, 1940-1986.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: Related materials: Kingman Brewster, Jr., President of Yale University, Records (RU 11); and Provost's Office, Yale University, Records (RU 92).

geogname.part: Great Britain -- Foreign relations -- United States
United States -- Foreign relations -- Great Britain

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series I consists chiefly of correspondence between Kingman Brewster and Mary Louise Phillips Brewster from 1941 (post-Yale graduation) to 1944, and correspondence to and from one or both of them covering 1946 to 1960 and 1977 to 1984. The most substantive correspondence is written by Kingman between 1941 and 1960. This correspondence documents his thoughts about his career, the development of his political philosophy, and early connections with persons, including Roger Baldwin, McGeorge Bundy, Ray Garrett Jr, and L. Douglas Heck, who later became significant figures in politics, law, and business. It also provides information about his tenure with the Economic Cooperation Administration. The 1977 to 1984 correspondence is mostly incoming and social in nature.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged under six subseries, primarily by family member: A. Kingman Brewster Jr., B. Mary Louise Phillips Brewster, C. Kingman Brewster and Mary Louise Phillips Brewster, D. Kingman Brewster, Sr., E. Other Family Correspondence, and F. Holiday Cards.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains incoming correspondence sent to Kingman Brewster, Jr.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains incoming correspondence sent to Mary Louise Phillips Brewster.

did.unittitle: Kingman Brewster, Jr. and Mary Louise Phillips Brewster

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes incoming and outgoing correspondence.

did.unittitle: Letters of condolence on the death of Kingman Brewster, Sr.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes incoming and outgoing correspondence.

did.unittitle: Kennedy, Mary, incoming, regarding the death of Mrs. Ballantine

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes correspondence pertaining to or kept by the Brewster family.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series II contains little information on Brewster's years as a Yale undergraduate and the months after graduation prior to his enlistment. The Pre-Military subseries, however, includes a copy of his Yale College senior essay. His 1941 class oration and several other speeches he gave while a Yale student, can be found in the Speeches files in Series IV. The Harvard, Economic Cooperation Association (ECA), and Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) subseries includes only scattered materials on the ECA and MIT; some MIT materials are also found in Harvard files. The subseries, however, incudes interesting materials, although limited in number, of Brewster's work towards establishing a world government organization. The Organizations and Family subseries document the more personal and social lives of the Brewster family.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged under five subseries: A. Pre-Military; B. Harvard, Economic Cooperation Association (ECA), and Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT); C. Harvard Law Student; D. Organizations; and E. Family.

scopecontent.p: Includes an admission ticket for an America First Committee meeting with Charles A. Lindbergh as the speaker, Woolsey Hall, 1940, and a press lounge pass for the 1940 Republican National Convention.

scopecontent.p: Includes an aerial photograph, service records, and Brewster's identification card.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes supporting documentation for honorary degrees received by Brewster from Columbia University, 1964; Dartmouth College, 1969; Dickinson College, 1969; University of Exeter, 1978; and University of Hull, 1979.

scopecontent.p: Includes buttons with Brewster's face, 1972, and an impeachment admission photograph.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes material that appears to have been retained due to Brewster's interest in the subjects.

did.unittitle: Vassar Club, New Haven, and other Vassar papers

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series III is arranged in four subseries that reflect the four divisions of Brewster's professional career. Brewster's interests in the international spheres of law, business, trade, and government is reflected throughout the Harvard Law Faculty subseries. The Straus Group and Economic Cooperation Administration files that document discussions on government financing for investment abroad are an excellent example, as are the files on the Organization for European Economic Cooperation that document relationships between government officials and foreign businessmen. His correspondence files, while including passing reference to personal or Law School administrative work, primarily focus on his teaching and research on the effect of U.S. anti-trust laws on U.S. businesses abroad. Brewster was an active faculty member and there are interesting files on work undertaken to revise the Harvard Law School curricula under "Legal education," and to study the interaction between law and behavioral sciences under "Ford study." There is substantive material on the activities of the American Association of University Professors at the national and locals level during his Harvard tenure. Brewster's files on the Civil Liberties Union of Massachusetts (CLUM) not only document his involvement in social justice issues, but provide an interesting snapshot of its work during the contentious McCarthy era. The order in which the files were received has been retained.

The small Yale subseries is arranged alphabetically and includes files of a personal nature and those on various organizations and societies to which Brewster belonged. For documentation of Brewster's years at Yale as provost and president, the researcher is directed to record groups in the Yale University Archives (see Related Materials note).

The General Files of the Ambassador subseries, arranged alphabetically, include calendars that when used with speeches and interviews from Series IV and VI provide a substantive overview of Brewster's public activities as Ambassador. There are many files throughout the series that document the places he visited and events he attended as ambassador. Correspondence files are mostly incoming and pertain primarily to social and personal matters.

The Post-Ambassador subseries, arranged alphabetically, provides a broad look at the kinds of issues Brewster dealt with when working with Winthrop, Stimson, Putnam, and Roberts. They demonstrate his continuing interests in American anti-trust laws and investments by businesses in areas other than their home countries. There are only a few files related to University College at Oxford, but they provide some insight into University financial matters he needed to address.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged under four subseries: A. Harvard Law School Faculty, B. Yale University, C. Ambassador, and D. Post-Ambassador.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Original audiovisual materials, as well as preservation and duplicating masters, may not be played. Researchers must consult use copies, or if none exist must pay for a use copy, which is retained by the repository. Researchers wishing to obtain an additional copy of non-commercially produced items for their personal use should consult the ordering reproductions information on the Manuscripts and Archives web site.

did.unittitle: Trades Union Congress, The History of the T.U.C. 1866-1968, A Pictorial Survey of a Social Revolution

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes event ephemera, photographs, and correspondence.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes materials documenting Brewster's work for the law firm of Winthrop, Stimson, Putnam, and Roberts and his mastership of University College at Oxford. This subseries occasionally includes materials created prior to Brewster's work at Winthrop, Stimson, Putnam, and Roberts and Oxford; these materials appear to have been filed by Brewster with his post-ambassador records.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes billing diaries, office manuals, benefits materials, billing procedures, and drafts of "Doing Business in the United States."

did.unittitle: Yale Center on International and Area Studies
Yale School of Art and School of Architecture commencement programs

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The most interesting materials in the Books subseries, arranged alphabetically by title, are drafts of an unpublished book by Brewster, "The Persistent Agenda of a Voluntary Society." The book, written by Brewster in the early 1980s, is a series of essays on how society can encourage freedom of choice for its citizens. It includes a revealing and informative autobiographical introduction. The Speeches subseries, arranged chronologically, documents the pre- and post-Yale years in Brewster's career and is quite extensive for his Ambassador years.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The series is arranged in four subseries: A. Articles, B. Books, C. Short Writings, and D. Speeches.

did.unittitle: "Enforceable Competition: Unruly Reason or Unreasonable Rules?" American Economic Review
"Pre-War Uncertainty," Yale Daily News 75th Anniversary Edition

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials are arranged as received from the donor.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Lengthy monograph, author unidentified, on applicability of United States anti-trust laws in foreign countries.

scopecontent.p: A book project by Kingman Brewster, Jr., apparently not published. Material in the first folder (box 253, folder 1) includes reader comments.

scopecontent.p: A book project by Kingman Brewster, Jr., apparently not published.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Original audiovisual materials, as well as preservation and duplicating masters, may not be played. Researchers must consult use copies, or if none exist must pay for a use copy, which is retained by the repository. Researchers wishing to obtain an additional copy of non-commercially produced items for their personal use should consult the ordering reproductions information on the Manuscripts and Archives web site.

Researchers wishing to obtain an additional copy of non-commercially produced items for their personal use should consult the ordering reproductions information on the Manuscripts and Archives web site.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series V includes photographs documenting Brewster's attendance of events and photographs of the Brewster family, including Kingman Brewster, Jr. and Mary Louise Phillips Brewster, as well as portraits of Kingman Brewster, Jr. Correspondence regarding the photographs is occasionally enclosed.The majority of the series documents family events and Brewster's years as ambassador. Many of the photographs lack specific descriptions.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The series is arranged under four headings: Family; Harvard Law School; People and events; and Portraits.

did.unittitle: Brewster family Christmas card photographs of Yale campus by Charles Alburtus
Harvard Law School faculty and Law Review
Ambassador event at the Savoy Hotel
Royal Society for the Encouragement of Arts, Manufactures, and Commerce
Space Shuttle Enterprise and its crew

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Along with Speeches in Series IV, materials in this series document Brewster's public persona as Ambassador. The Clippings are mostly arranged alphabetically by publication title and are reflections on Brewster's ambassadorship by British journalists and commentators. The Interviews subseries consists of audio and video-recorded interviews and are arranged chronologically. They only cover his Ambassador years, but are quite extensive. Along with his speeches, they are valuable in documenting how Brewster shaped conversations in Britain about the United States in world affairs and the relationship between the United States and Great Britain.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged under two headings: Clippings and Interviews.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Original audiovisual materials, as well as preservation and duplicating masters, may not be played. Researchers must consult use copies, or if none exist must pay for a use copy, which is retained by the repository. Researchers wishing to obtain an additional copy of non-commercially produced items for their personal use should consult the ordering reproductions information on the Manuscripts and Archives web site.
